content:
Practical lessons that instantly improved the quality of my code

4 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I had just finished what I thought was a “simple” feature for a client dashboard. Nothing fancy just a few buttons, a couple of API calls, and some dynamic UI updates. I refreshed the page, clicked a button, and everything worked.
Then I clicked it again.
The button fired twice.
A few seconds later, the page froze. My console looked like a crime scene. Duplicate requests everywhere. Random values showing up. Event listeners multiplying faster than I could delete them.
I leaned back in my chair, laughed for a second, and realized something uncomfortable.
The problem wasn’t JavaScript.
It was the way I had been writing JavaScript.
Over the past four years, I’ve built everything from automation tools and internal dashboards to portfolio websites and developer utilities. During that time, I’ve rewritten thousands of lines of JavaScript not because they were broken, but because they were unnecessarily complicated.
Eventually, I noticed something interesting.
